articles.title: What is Inflation and How Does It Affect Us?
articles.summary: Inflation means prices are going up, and it's at 3.3% in the UK right now. This is partly because of a war far away that makes fuel more expensive. Fuel is used for cars and heating homes, so when it costs more, everything gets pricier. But not all prices are rising—energy bills might go down a bit this month, saving families about £10. Airfares went up because of Easter, but they could drop soon. Experts think inflation might fall below 3% in April but could go up to 4% later this year. This affects everyone because it makes buying food and traveling harder. It's important to watch these changes to understand how money works.

articles.title: Lufthansa Reduces Summer Flights Due to High Fuel Costs
articles.summary: Lufthansa, a German airline, is cutting 20,000 short flights this summer because fuel prices have gone up a lot. Fuel costs more because of a war in the Middle East that makes it hard to produce and move fuel. This war involves the US, Israel, and Iran. Other airlines like KLM-France and Delta are also cutting some flights or making tickets more expensive. Experts say travelers might see more flight cancellations and higher ticket prices. Lufthansa says this change will save fuel and help them run more efficiently. They are also closing some routes in Europe and retiring old planes. The war affects fuel supply because much of Europe's fuel comes from the Gulf region, and Iran has blocked a key shipping route. Even if the war ends, it will take time for things to go back to normal.

articles.title: Runners Help Tired Man Finish Marathon
articles.summary: At the Boston Marathon, a runner named Ajay Haridasse fell down near the finish line and couldn't get up. He was very tired and tried to stand but kept falling. Many runners passed by, but two kind runners, Aaron Beggs and Robson De Oliveira, stopped to help him. They put their arms around Ajay and helped him walk to the finish line. They did this even though it made their own race times slower. People watching cheered and filmed the nice moment. Ajay thanked the runners for helping him when he needed it most. This story shows how important it is to help others.

articles.title: Texas Schools Can Show the Ten Commandments
articles.summary: A court in Texas said that public schools can put up the Ten Commandments in classrooms. This is a win for some people who want more religion in schools. The Ten Commandments are rules from the Bible. The court said this does not break the First Amendment, which is a law that protects religious freedom and keeps the government from making an official religion. Texas leaders are happy, but groups like the ACLU are upset. They think it goes against the separation of church and state, meaning religion and government should stay apart. This might go to the Supreme Court later. The law was made by Republicans, and some people argue the Ten Commandments are important for history and U.S. law.

articles.title: President Trump Talks to College Champions at the White House
articles.summary: President Donald Trump spoke to college athletes at the White House. He honored about 100 athletes from seven teams that won national championships in 2025. The teams included Oklahoma State in men's golf, Texas A&M in women's volleyball, Wake Forest in men's tennis, Georgia in women's tennis, Youngstown State in women's bowling, Florida State in women's soccer, and West Virginia in mixed rifle. Trump talked about new rules for college sports. He wants Congress to make laws to help control how athletes move between schools and get paid. He signed an order to limit how long athletes can play and protect women's sports. Some people think these rules might not be legal, so they need laws to make them stronger. Trump said it's not fair for young athletes to compete against older ones.

articles.title: Justice Department Accuses SPLC of Fraud Over Informant Payments
articles.summary: The Justice Department has charged the Southern Poverty Law Center (SPLC), a civil rights group, with fraud. The government says the SPLC raised money from donors to fight extremist groups like the Ku Klux Klan but instead used that money to pay informants inside those groups. This happened from 2014 to 2023, with at least $3 million paid. The SPLC says it used the program to gather information on threats and share it with police. The charges include wire fraud and money laundering. The SPLC is based in Alabama and plans to defend itself in court.
